chain_of_thought: Top native frame is Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, which we decompiled. No allocators/sanitizers above it in the trace, so this is the first app-level frame.

Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum takes a long param_1 assumed to be pointer to an ASN1 structure. It immediately calls Compute_ASN1_ValueLength() with no arguments (likely uses global or fields of param_1) and then iterates over bytes in *(long *)(param_1+0x10).

Loop logic: iVar6 accumulates offset, compared to total length iVar2. For each iteration, it reads bVar1 from base+offset, inspects tag bits, then increments offset, computes length size and value length via helper functions, updates counters. If value length == -1 or tag class invalid → early exit returning current child count (or 0).

Potential risks in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum: out-of-bounds read of *(byte *)(*(long *)(param_1+0x10)+iVar6) or of subsequent Compute_ASN1_LengthSize / ValueLength_from_LO if iVar6 ever exceeds iVar2 or if param_1+0x10 or its dereferenced pointer is invalid. However, the function uses iVar6 and iVar2 (value length) relationship: it stops when iVar6 == iVar2. No explicit guard against iVar6 > iVar2, but iVar6 only increases by iVar4+iVar5+1 where iVar4/iVar5 are derived from the buffer itself; malformed ASN.1 could make them large causing iVar6 to skip past iVar2, but the loop condition is `if (iVar6 == iVar2) break;` executed after updating, so when iVar6 becomes > iVar2 it will never equal, and the loop continues indefinitely until possibly overflow. However, crash type given is not SIGSEGV but a symbolic Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum termination, so actual signal unknown.

Within Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT, the first action after parameter validation is `iVar1 = Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum(param_1);` and then it requires `iVar1 >= 8` and that certain Get_DER_Child calls succeed. If Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum misbehaves on malformed ASN.1, TBS conversion will likely just fail via its own checks and cleanup. There is no memcpy/memmove in Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT itself; memory is allocated with calloc, and error paths are careful: Delete_ASN1/Delete_BINT/etc and free only allocations that are non-null.

Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT constructs an X509_CERT from ASN1, delegating to Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT. It validates parameters, creates a cert if needed, and on failure frees appropriately. No memcpy/unsafe operations here.

CERT_Load wraps Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT, again with simple control logic and no raw buffer ops. If conversion fails it returns error codes without freeing param_2 (BINSTR) because BINSTR lifetime is external.

JNI entry Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID: this is the true attacker entry because fuzz harness calls it. It calls function pointers in JNIEnv (via *param_1 + offsets) to get the length of a Java byte array (iVar1), cast to size_t __size, then obtains a direct pointer __src to the Java bytes, allocates malloc(__size), memset(__s,0,__size), and memcpy(__s,__src,__size). No null check on malloc return; for size 0 it will malloc(0) which is valid, __s may be NULL but memcpy with size 0 would still be safe; here though memcpy is always called with length __size, so if __size is non-zero and malloc fails (returns NULL), memcpy will dereference NULL → crash. However, malloc failure at fuzz sizes is adversarial but in production on mobile this still can happen under memory pressure, but this is more robustness/DoS, not exploitable memory corruption, and here the fuzzer is in-process; more importantly, fuzzer likely uses small sizes so malloc() will not fail. The crash described refers to Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, not memcpy NULL; no ASAN/SEGV details so likely some other issue.

After copying bytes into __s, they create a BINSTR and set its data to __s with length iVar1. CERT_Load parses the DER and builds certificate structures. On success, they clear a stack buffer of 0x400 bytes and call CERT_GetCertPolicy_PolicyID(local_420,0x400,auStack_408); this API takes explicit size, so policy ID writing is bounded by 1024 bytes, preventing overflow of the stack buffer.

Important: there is a memory leak on failure paths in Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID: if CERT_Load or CERT_GetCertPolicy_PolicyID fail (return !=1), they do not free __s or delete BINSTR/local_418/local_420. But the fuzzer crash occurs earlier in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, so leak is unrelated.

Now about taint / attacker influence: Java input byte array completely controls the contents of __s, which is passed via BINSTR_SetData and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST into ASN1 structures. Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum then interprets this ASN1 data. Its only parameter param_1 is ASN1 tree pointer coming from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, which is derived from attacker bytes. That means malformed ASN1 could stress Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum. We must identify any unsafe read/write based on the attacker-controlled structure.

In Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, the only direct memory access is reading a byte at offset iVar6 in buffer *(long *)(param_1+0x10). There is no write. Helper functions Compute_ASN1_LengthSize and Compute_ASN1_ValueLength_from_LO operate on the same buffer; without their definitions we cannot prove absence of out-of-bounds, but the typical design is they validate lengths carefully. This function itself uses Compute_ASN1_ValueLength() (no args) to get total value length; presumably the ASN1 object already contains correct length, and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST likely ensures internal consistency or at least stores lengths so that Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum cannot walk beyond allocated buffers. Even if value length field in DER is malformed/too big, Decode_DER_ASN1_ST is responsible for constraining it when constructing the internal representation. We do not see Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum trusting DER length fields directly; it trusts the internal ASN1 object.

Crash type given is just the function name, not a specific memory error. Stack trace shows Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum at the top; could be due to it returning 0 or negative, causing caller to treat that as assertion failure? However, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT just checks `if (iVar1 < 8) goto error;` and does not crash. Therefore likely the fuzzer recorded any abort inside this function as a crash, but we have no evidence of deref of NULL or wild pointer there.

We must also consider integer issues: iVar6, iVar4, iVar5, iVar7, iVar2 are 32-bit ints, whereas offsets and buffer lengths might be larger; but ASN1 sizes are usually limited and BINSTR_SetData likely sets length to iVar1 which is int length of Java byte array; this can be up to around 2^31-1, but realistic certificates are small (<10KB). Fuzzer may generate large arrays, but size_t and int conversions happen: iVar1 = GetArrayLength; __size = (size_t)iVar1; BINSTR_SetData(__s, iVar1, &local_418); So internal length is int. Even if huge, Decode_DER_ASN1_ST would fail long before allocations or loops exceed 32-bit boundaries, or will return 0; CERT_Load checks for 0 and aborts. No loop in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum uses unbounded index; while potentially infinite for extremely malformed data (iVar6 not reaching iVar2) but iVar6 increments at least by 1 each iteration, so for finite iVar2 it will eventually pass equality test or overflow; but overflow of signed int iVar6 could wrap negative and break assumption. However, overflow would require pathologically large iVar2 and/or lengths; not realistic for typical mobile inputs and would more likely be limited by Decode_DER_ASN1_ST which must allocate objects accordingly.

Given available code, there is no explicit unchecked memcpy using attacker-controlled size except JNI memcpy, which copies from a Java-managed buffer of length __size into a malloc buffer of same size. This is safe with respect to overflows because destination is allocated exactly that size. Risk is only NULL deref if malloc returns NULL for big sizes, which is more a resource exhaustion / DoS than code corruption. The fuzzer-induced crash at Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum does not show such pattern. There is no evidence of a concrete memory safety bug or exploitable state reachable by realistic use cases.

Therefore, this crash is most likely due to malformed ASN1 causing internal decode/parsing failure, which then cascades to an early return or perhaps an assertion internal to Decode_DER_ASN1_ST or associated functions, but we cannot see any such assert in the decompiled functions we have. Without a concrete unsafe operation directly tied to attacker-controlled values, we should not flag this as a vulnerability. It may reflect robust handling (returning 0 / error codes) rather than a bug.

Conclusion: classify as not a genuine vulnerability. Impact is at most a local denial-of-service via app crash on extremely malformed or enormous certificate data, but given the defensive checks and lack of direct OOB writes/reads, and absence of clear crash mechanism, confidence in non-vulnerability is moderately high.
reasons: Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum only performs bounded byte reads based on an internal ASN1 length; no writes or obvious out-of-bounds access are visible in this function.

The JNI entry copies attacker-controlled bytes with memcpy into a correctly sized malloc buffer; the only risk is malloc(NULL) failure leading to NULL deref, which is a generic DoS, not memory corruption, and unrelated to the observed top frame.

Certificate parsing pipeline (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT/ CERT_Load) consistently checks return values and cleans up; malformed ASN.1 will cause error returns, not unsafe behavior, so the fuzz crash likely reflects parser rejection or harness conditions, not an exploitable bug.
recommendations: Instrument runs with ASan/UBSan or hardware watchpoints to capture an exact signal (SIGSEGV, abort) and confirm there is no hidden out-of-bounds in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST or Compute_ASN1_LengthSize/ValueLength_from_LO.
Add null-check for malloc(__size) in Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID and free/BINSTR_Delete/CERT_Unload on all error paths to harden against DoS and memory leaks.
Optionally cap maximum accepted certificate/DER size at the JNI boundary to avoid heavy allocations or pathological parser behavior under fuzzing.
assumptions: Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and Compute_ASN1_LengthSize/Compute_ASN1_ValueLength_from_LO correctly validate lengths against allocated buffer sizes.
The recorded crash reason (“Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum”) does not hide an underlying allocator/sanitizer abort such as ASan-detected OOB access.
Typical production inputs (certificates) remain within reasonable size bounds (e.g., <64KB).
limitations: Implementations of Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, Compute_ASN1_LengthSize, and Compute_ASN1_ValueLength_from_LO are not visible, so hidden issues in them cannot be fully ruled out.
The exact signal or exception causing the crash is not provided; analysis relies on stack trace and decompiled code structure.
No runtime traces or sanitizer logs are available to validate that the loop in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum never oversteps buffer boundaries.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is __memcpy; first app-level frame below it is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST in libUbikeyJni.so, which we decompiled.
Within Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, the memcpy call is clearly visible: destination is a malloc(__size) buffer stored in puVar4[2], source is param_1 + iVar10, and length is __size.

__size is derived from the DER length field taken directly from input bytes param_1. For short form lengths, __size = (size_t)bVar2 where bVar2 = param_1[1]. For long form, __size is computed from additional bytes; there is a guard that rejects negative (signed) values but no upper bound or sanity cap.

As long as malloc(__size) succeeds, destination and length are consistent (both use __size). This makes classic heap-buffer overflow via memcpy unlikely; if malloc returns NULL but __size>0, code goes to error path without memcpy, so no NULL-deref there.

The fuzz crash in __memcpy is therefore most plausibly due to allocator or environment-side failure (e.g., corrupted heap from elsewhere, extremely large allocation being partially satisfied in fuzzing, or sanitizer-injected abort), not directly from an out-of-bounds in this function, since memcpy dst/src/len arguments are internally consistent and under app control, not attacker-controlled pointers.

At the JNI entry (Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID), there is another memcpy: dst is malloc(__size); src is fetched via a function pointer from param_1 (JNI env / app context); len is __size = (size_t)iVar1 where iVar1 is obtained via another function pointer on param_1, presumably returning the blob length. Since src and len come from the same provider, this is also internally consistent unless that provider itself has a bug. The fuzz harness can likely make these function pointers return inconsistent values, which would not be reachable in real app use.

CERT_Load and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT use Decode_DER_ASN1_ST on controlled inputs and do not introduce additional memcpy with mismatched sizes; they primarily call other helpers and manage pointers.

Given the available code, there is no obvious out-of-bounds write/read or use-after-free directly tied to attacker-controlled size vs. buffer size in these paths. Crashes in memcpy during fuzzing are common when the harness feeds unrealistic or truncated data or misconfigures JNI context, especially with function pointer callbacks that are opaque here.

Thus, while this is ASN.1 parsing logic with dynamic length handling (a class of code that often has vulnerabilities), the specific crash lacks evidence of a concrete, reachable memory safety flaw. The memcpy arguments are coherently derived and checked for basic negativity; no under-sized buffer is apparent.

I therefore classify the issue as not a confirmed vulnerability, leaning towards an environmental/harness artifact, with moderate confidence because we cannot see the implementations behind the indirect callbacks and malloc behavior.
reasons: Decompiled Decode_DER_ASN1_ST shows memcpy(dst,param_1+iVar10,__size) where dst is malloc(__size); length and buffer size match, avoiding classic overflow in this function.

JNI entry Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID also uses memcpy into a buffer sized exactly by the same length value it passes, so mismatch-based overflow is unlikely unless underlying callbacks misbehave.

Crash likely stems from fuzz harness or opaque callback behavior rather than a clear, app-level memory corruption primitive; no direct attacker-controlled out-of-bounds or UAF is evident in the visible code.
recommendations: Instrument and re-run fuzzing with ASan/UBSan and full stack traces to confirm whether memcpy arguments are ever inconsistent (dst size < len).
Add explicit upper bounds/sanity checks on ASN.1 length fields before malloc to prevent excessive allocations and potential DoS, even if not exploitable for memory corruption.
Review and fuzz the implementations behind the function-pointer callbacks off param_1 (JNI/env object) to ensure they always return consistent pointers and lengths.
assumptions: malloc behaves normally and returns either a valid buffer of requested size or NULL without partial allocations.
Opaque callbacks accessed via (*param_1+0x558/0x5c0/0x600) are part of the JNI/env layer and not attacker-controlled in a way that breaks pointer/length consistency in production.
Fuzz harness may be passing unrealistic or invalid JNI/env states that do not occur in real app flows.
limitations: Implementations of the JNI-related function pointers are not visible, so misbehavior there cannot be fully excluded.
Heap state and exact memcpy operands at crash time are unknown; assessment is based solely on static decompilation.
Other parts of libUbikeyJni.so or other libraries invoked indirectly might corrupt memory before these functions run, which is not analyzed here.
